title: Data Analysis and Statistics for Political Science
text:
Preface





This is a book primarily for undergraduate students taking a course in data analysis and statistics. Many statistics and data science books are geared toward general audiences or statistics students. Those books typically do not contain many political science examples. This book is designed to gives students a foundation in data analysis and statistics but with explicitly political science examples. The use of examples helps solidify complicated concepts surrounding statistics. Political science students will relate to these political science examples and facilitate mastery of the content.
Book outline

title: About the Author
text: Brandon L. Bartels is a Professor of Political Science at George Washington University. His research and teaching interests center on American politics, judicial politics, the U.S. Supreme Court, public perceptions of law and courts, institutional legitimacy, and judicial power and court-curbing in comparative context. Brandon’s work has appeared in the American Political Science Review, American Journal of Political Science, Journal of Politics, Public Opinion Quarterly, and other outlets. My research has also been supported by the National Science Foundation. His book with Christopher D. Johnston, Curbing the Court: Why the Public Constrains Judicial Independence, was published in 2020 by Cambridge University Press.
